text: What is the main reason proteins can have so many different functions?
choices: They are always made of identical monomers in the same sequence
Their amino acid sequences and resulting three-dimensional shapes can vary greatly
They contain only carbon and hydrogen
They are the only biological molecules that can form polymers
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Protein diversity comes from variation in amino acid sequence and resulting shape.
Correct. Different sequences lead to different folds, binding abilities, and functions.
Incorrect. Proteins also contain nitrogen and often sulfur and oxygen.
Incorrect. Nucleic acids and carbohydrates can also form polymers.
explain: Different primary sequences produce different folding patterns and functional surfaces.

text: What is the main function of hydrogen bonds between complementary DNA bases?
choices: They create the primary structure of a protein
They help hold the two DNA strands together
They link nucleotides together in one strand
They attach fatty acids to glycerol
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Protein primary structure is formed by peptide bonds.
Correct. Hydrogen bonds between complementary bases stabilize the double helix.
Incorrect. Nucleotides within one strand are linked by phosphodiester bonds.
Incorrect. Fatty acids are attached to glycerol by ester linkages.
explain: Hydrogen bonds provide specificity and stability between base pairs across the two strands.

text: A researcher compares two amino acid substitutions in a protein. Mutation 1 replaces a nonpolar amino acid in the protein interior with another nonpolar amino acid. Mutation 2 replaces the same original amino acid with a charged amino acid. Which prediction is most reasonable?
choices: Mutation 2 is more likely to disrupt folding because the interior of many proteins favors nonpolar side chains
Mutation 1 is more disruptive because all amino acid changes affect folding equally
Mutation 2 will convert the protein into DNA
Mutation 1 must eliminate all tertiary structure
choice_explanations: Correct. Charged residues in the hydrophobic core can strongly disrupt normal packing and stability.
Incorrect. Amino acid changes do not all have equal structural impact.
Incorrect. Amino acid substitutions do not change the molecule class.
Incorrect. Replacing one nonpolar residue with another is often less disruptive.
explain: Introducing charge into a hydrophobic core destabilizes packing and folding.

text: A solution becomes more acidic after metabolism in a cell. Which cellular feature would be most helpful for limiting damage to proteins?
choices: Buffer molecules that can minimize large changes in hydrogen ion concentration
More triglycerides stored in lipid droplets
Fewer peptide bonds in proteins
Converting all proteins into carbohydrates
choice_explanations: Correct. Buffers help stabilize pH and protect molecules whose structure depends on charge interactions.
Incorrect. Triglyceride storage does not directly stabilize pH.
Incorrect. Peptide bond number is not the main issue in pH buffering.
Incorrect. Cells do not solve acidity this way.
explain: Buffers resist changes in pH and help maintain ionization states important for protein structure.

text: A scientist synthesizes two lipids. Lipid A has three fatty acid tails attached to glycerol. Lipid B has two fatty acid tails and a phosphate-containing head group. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: Lipid A is better suited for membrane bilayers than Lipid B
Lipid B is amphipathic and better suited for membrane structure
Lipid A is a nucleic acid because it has three parts
Lipid B must be a protein because it contains phosphorus
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Triglyceride-like molecules are not as well suited for bilayer formation as phospholipids.
Correct. The phosphate-containing head creates a hydrophilic region while the tails are hydrophobic.
Incorrect. Nucleic acids are built from nucleotides, not glycerol and fatty acids.
Incorrect. Presence of phosphorus does not make a molecule a protein.
explain: Phospholipids are amphipathic and assemble into bilayers; triglycerides form storage droplets.

text: A carbohydrate polymer is easily broken down for energy in animal cells. Another carbohydrate polymer made of the same monomer is not digested by the same enzymes. Which explanation is best?
choices: The polymers likely differ in glycosidic linkage arrangement or overall shape
One polymer must contain amino acids
Digestive enzymes act equally on all sugar polymers
The indigestible polymer must actually be a lipid
choice_explanations: Correct. Enzyme specificity depends on exact bond orientation and structure.
Incorrect. The prompt states both are carbohydrate polymers of the same monomer.
Incorrect. Enzymes are selective.
Incorrect. The prompt identifies both as carbohydrate polymers.
explain: Differences in linkage type and polymer architecture determine enzyme accessibility.

text: A mutation in DNA changes a codon so that the amino acid inserted into a protein is smaller and less polar than before. The change occurs at a substrate-binding site. Which outcome is most likely?
choices: Substrate binding may weaken because the site’s shape or chemistry has changed
The protein will automatically gain more peptide bonds
The protein must become longer
The DNA will lose all hydrogen bonds
choice_explanations: Correct. Even one amino acid change in a binding site can alter substrate fit and interactions.
Incorrect. A codon substitution does not add extra peptide bonds.
Incorrect. A substitution does not necessarily change protein length.
Incorrect. One codon change does not eliminate all DNA hydrogen bonds.
explain: Altered side-chain size or polarity can change binding affinity and catalytic efficiency.

text: A researcher measures how much heat is needed to raise the temperature of equal volumes of two liquids. Liquid X requires much more heat than Liquid Y. What is the best conclusion?
choices: Liquid X likely has a higher specific heat and resists temperature change more strongly
Liquid Y must contain more hydrogen bonds
Liquid X cannot absorb thermal energy
Liquid Y must be a protein solution
choice_explanations: Correct. Requiring more heat for the same temperature increase means a higher specific heat.
Incorrect. The data do not directly prove that.
Incorrect. It absorbs thermal energy; it just changes temperature less.
Incorrect. The result does not identify the exact composition.
explain: Specific heat describes how much energy is needed per unit temperature change.

text: A DNA-binding protein has several positively charged amino acids on its surface. Why might these be especially important?
choices: They can interact favorably with the negatively charged phosphate backbone of DNA
They make the protein behave like a triglyceride
They prevent the protein from ever folding
They replace the need for peptide bonds
choice_explanations: Correct. Electrostatic attraction often helps DNA-binding proteins interact with nucleic acids.
Incorrect. Surface charge does not make a protein a lipid.
Incorrect. Charged residues can contribute to normal folding and function.
Incorrect. Peptide bonds are still required for protein structure.
explain: Positive surface charges complement DNA’s negatively charged backbone and assist binding.

text: A biologist finds that a membrane protein has many hydrophobic amino acids facing outward. Which environment most likely surrounds that protein surface?
choices: The watery cytoplasm
The hydrophobic interior of a phospholipid bilayer
Blood plasma only
A starch granule
choice_explanations: Incorrect. Hydrophobic outward-facing residues are less favorable in aqueous environments.
Correct. Nonpolar residues are well suited to interact with membrane interiors.
Incorrect. Blood plasma is aqueous.
Incorrect. Protein surface properties do not suggest association with starch here.
explain: Hydrophobic surfaces are compatible with the nonpolar membrane interior.

text: A researcher breaks down an unknown polymer. The monomers contain a central carbon attached to an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and a variable side chain. Which conclusion is most supported?
choices: The polymer was a protein
The polymer was a nucleic acid
The polymer was a phospholipid
The polymer was glycogen
choice_explanations: Correct. That monomer description identifies amino acids.
Incorrect. Nucleic acids are built from nucleotides, not amino acids.
Incorrect. Phospholipids are not amino-acid polymers.
Incorrect. Glycogen is a glucose polymer.
explain: Amino acids have the described backbone and variable side chain.

text: A student states that water’s cohesion and high specific heat both result from hydrogen bonding. Which response is best?
choices: The student is correct because hydrogen bonding underlies both properties
The student is incorrect because hydrogen bonding affects only cohesion
The student is incorrect because water is nonpolar
The student is correct only if salts are dissolved in the water
choice_explanations: Correct. Hydrogen bonds help water molecules stick together and resist rapid temperature changes.
Incorrect. Hydrogen bonding also contributes to high specific heat.
Incorrect. Water is polar.
Incorrect. These are intrinsic properties of water itself.
explain: Hydrogen bonding is central to both cohesion and water’s heat capacity.

text: A scientist compares two DNA molecules of equal length. Molecule A has many more G-C base pairs than Molecule B. Which prediction is most reasonable?
choices: Molecule A may require more energy to separate into single strands
Molecule A must be RNA
Molecule B cannot replicate
Molecule A lacks phosphodiester bonds
choice_explanations: Correct. G-C pairs form three hydrogen bonds, increasing stability.
Incorrect. G-C content does not make a molecule RNA.
Incorrect. Lower G-C content does not prevent replication.
Incorrect. DNA strands contain phosphodiester bonds regardless of base composition.
explain: Higher G-C percentage often increases duplex thermal stability.

text: An enzyme catalyzes a reaction best at pH 7, but its activity drops at pH 3 and pH 10. Which explanation is best?
choices: Extreme pH likely alters side-chain charges and disrupts active-site structure or substrate interaction
The enzyme works poorly because all peptide bonds break at pH 3 and pH 10
The enzyme becomes a lipid at extreme pH
pH affects only the substrate and never the enzyme
choice_explanations: Correct. pH affects ionization states and can change enzyme shape and binding.
Incorrect. Loss of activity usually reflects denaturation or altered interactions, not total peptide bond destruction.
Incorrect. Proteins do not convert into lipids because of pH.
Incorrect. pH can affect both.
explain: Activity peaks at the enzyme’s pH optimum where ionizable groups support catalysis.

text: A cell must store energy compactly for long periods. Which macromolecule type is generally best suited for this role?
choices: Lipids, because they contain many energy-rich carbon-hydrogen bonds
Proteins, because they are always the main energy reserve
Nucleic acids, because they are highly nonpolar
Water, because it contains hydrogen
choice_explanations: Correct. Lipids are efficient long-term energy storage molecules.
Incorrect. Proteins are not usually the primary long-term energy storage form.
Incorrect. Nucleic acids primarily store genetic information.
Incorrect. Water is not an energy storage macromolecule.
explain: Lipids have dense C–H bonds that yield high energy upon oxidation.

text: A protein with multiple subunits loses function when one subunit no longer associates with the others after mutation. Which level of protein structure was most directly affected?
choices: Primary structure only
Secondary structure only
Quaternary structure
Phosphodiester structure
choice_explanations: Incorrect. The issue specifically involves interaction among subunits.
Incorrect. Secondary structure refers to local folding patterns.
Correct. Quaternary structure involves the arrangement of multiple polypeptide subunits.
Incorrect. Proteins do not have phosphodiester structure.
explain: Loss of subunit association disrupts quaternary-level organization and function.

text: A researcher finds two molecules with identical molecular formulas but different biological roles. What best explains this observation?
choices: Molecules can be isomers with different arrangements of atoms and therefore different properties
Molecules with the same formula must always behave identically
One of the molecules must not contain carbon
Biological role depends only on molecular mass
choice_explanations: Correct. Structural differences can lead to different functions.
Incorrect. Same formula does not guarantee same structure or role.
Incorrect. If the formulas are identical, their elemental composition matches.
Incorrect. Structure and chemistry matter more than mass alone.
explain: Isomerism changes geometry or bonding while preserving formula, altering properties.

text: A phospholipid-rich membrane is cooled, and later the membrane becomes less fluid. Which structural explanation is most relevant?
choices: Lower temperature reduces molecular movement and allows tighter packing of the tails
Cooling removes all hydrophilic head groups
Cooling converts phospholipids into proteins
Cooling breaks every covalent bond in the membrane
choice_explanations: Correct. Cooling generally decreases membrane fluidity.
Incorrect. Temperature does not remove the head groups.
Incorrect. Temperature change does not change lipid class.
Incorrect. Reduced fluidity is not caused by total bond breakage.
explain: Temperature affects kinetic energy and how tightly lipid tails pack together.

text: A mutation replaces a cysteine with a different amino acid that cannot form disulfide bonds. Which outcome is most likely in some proteins?
choices: Reduced structural stability because a covalent stabilizing interaction may be lost
Immediate conversion of the protein into DNA
Elimination of all hydrogen bonds in the protein
Automatic increase in protein length
choice_explanations: Correct. Disulfide bonds can strengthen tertiary or quaternary structure.
Incorrect. Proteins do not change into DNA this way.
Incorrect. One cysteine substitution does not remove all hydrogen bonds.
Incorrect. A substitution does not necessarily change length.
explain: Loss of a disulfide bond can reduce protein stability in some contexts.

text: A researcher hydrolyzes a nucleic acid and finds one monomer with ribose and uracil. Which conclusion is best?
choices: The monomer came from RNA
The monomer came from DNA
The monomer came from a triglyceride
The monomer came from cellulose
choice_explanations: Correct. RNA nucleotides contain ribose and uracil.
Incorrect. DNA contains deoxyribose and thymine.
Incorrect. Triglycerides are lipids, not nucleotide polymers.
Incorrect. Cellulose is a glucose polymer.
explain: Ribose + uracil indicates an RNA nucleotide.

text:
Students compare four unknown samples with indicator tests.

Sample A:
Benedict’s positive
Biuret negative
Iodine negative

Sample B:
Benedict’s negative
Biuret positive
Iodine negative

Sample C:
Benedict’s negative
Biuret negative
Iodine positive

Sample D:
Benedict’s negative
Biuret negative
Forms an oily translucent spot on paper

Which matching is most likely?
choices: A = reducing sugar, B = protein, C = starch, D = lipid
A = protein, B = lipid, C = reducing sugar, D = starch
A = starch, B = reducing sugar, C = protein, D = nucleic acid
A = lipid, B = starch, C = protein, D = reducing sugar
choice_explanations: Correct. Each indicator result matches the typical test for those biomolecules.
Incorrect. The test patterns do not match that assignment.
Incorrect. This reverses the Benedict’s, Biuret, and iodine interpretations.
Incorrect. The observed tests do not support that ordering.
explain: Benedict's indicates reducing sugars, Biuret indicates peptide bonds (proteins), iodine indicates starch, oily spot indicates lipids.

text: A scientist studies two related enzymes. One enzyme has a negatively charged amino acid at a binding site and binds a positively charged substrate strongly. A mutation changes that amino acid to a positively charged one, and binding weakens. Which explanation is best?
choices: The mutation likely disrupted an electrostatic attraction that helped binding
The mutation must have lengthened the enzyme
The mutation proves primary structure is unimportant
The enzyme has become a lipid
choice_explanations: Correct. Opposite charges attract; changing to the same charge can weaken interaction.
Incorrect. A single substitution does not necessarily change length.
Incorrect. The scenario shows the opposite.
Incorrect. A protein does not become a lipid because of one amino acid substitution.
explain: Electrostatic complementarity often contributes to binding specificity.

text:
A class measures pH after adding the same small amount of acid to two solutions.

Solution X: pH changes from 7.0 to 6.8
Solution Y: pH changes from 7.0 to 4.9

Which conclusion is best supported?
choices: Solution X has greater buffering capacity
Solution Y has greater buffering capacity
Solution X contains no water
Solution Y must contain more proteins
choice_explanations: Correct. Smaller pH change indicates better resistance to added acid.
Incorrect. Large pH change means weaker buffering.
Incorrect. The data do not suggest that.
Incorrect. The result only supports a conclusion about pH buffering, not protein amount.
explain: Buffers stabilize pH changes; a smaller shift indicates stronger buffering in the tested range.

text: Researchers compare two proteins with the same amino acid length. Protein A contains many hydrophilic residues on the outside and functions in cytoplasm. Protein B contains many hydrophobic residues on the outside and functions in a membrane. Which principle is most directly supported?
choices: Protein surface chemistry is shaped by the environment in which the protein functions
All proteins with the same length behave the same way
Membrane proteins are made of phospholipids rather than amino acids
Cytoplasmic proteins cannot contain nonpolar residues at all
choice_explanations: Correct. Stable and functional proteins often match their surroundings chemically.
Incorrect. Length alone does not determine function.
Incorrect. Membrane proteins are still proteins.
Incorrect. They often contain nonpolar residues internally.
explain: Surface residue polarity tends to reflect the protein’s working environment.

text: A DNA sample with high G-C content is compared with one with lower G-C content. The high G-C sample requires a higher temperature for strand separation. Which explanation is best supported?
choices: Greater G-C content increases hydrogen bonding and often raises thermal stability
High G-C DNA must be RNA
The lower G-C DNA has no sugar-phosphate backbone
Only proteins are affected by heating
choice_explanations: Correct. G-C base pairs contribute more hydrogen bonds per pair.
Incorrect. G-C content does not determine whether a molecule is RNA.
Incorrect. Both DNA samples have sugar-phosphate backbones.
Incorrect. Nucleic acid strand separation also depends on heat.
explain: G-C pairs form three hydrogen bonds versus two for A-T, increasing stability and melting temperature.
